content.text: A retrospective that produces action items without a mechanism to verify their completion is not a continuous improvement process — it is a recurring complaint session. The follow-through problem is universal: studies of team retrospectives consistently find that fewer than half of action items from one retro are completed before the next. For GenAI teams, where the improvement backlog competes with feature delivery and model migration work, the completion rate is often lower. This sub-subtopic covers the system for tracking, escalating, and learning from retro action items.

attrs.alt: Retro action item lifecycle: Created → In Progress → Verified Done → Closed, with escape paths for Overdue, Deferred, and Cancelled states; health metrics panel showing on-time completion rate, open item age, and deferral rate thresholds

content.text: Why Action Items Fail
The same failure modes appear across teams regardless of methodology:
No single owner.
"The team" will update the eval process means no one will update the eval process. Every action item must have exactly one named person who is accountable for completion.
Vague completion criteria.
"Improve prompt documentation" cannot be verified closed. "Add a system-prompt section to the README with the current rationale and the eval score when it was last updated" can be.
No due date before the next retro.

Action items with far-future due dates create false comfort. If the work cannot be done before the next retro, either the scope is too large (break it down) or it is not actually a priority (cancel it).
No review at the next retro.
If previous action items are not reviewed at the opening of the next retro, the implicit message is that follow-through does not matter. This signal is absorbed immediately and permanently.
Action items batched into a separate system.
Retro action items tracked in a separate spreadsheet or doc from the team's regular work become invisible. They must live in the same system as sprint tickets to compete for attention.
The Action Item Schema
Every action item — from sprint retro, quality retro, architecture retro, or safety retro — is stored with the same schema. Consistency enables cross-retro reporting and trend detection:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

RetroSource = Literal["sprint", "quality", "architecture", "safety", "cross-team-forum"]
ActionStatus = Literal["open", "in_progress", "done", "deferred", "cancelled"]

@dataclass
class RetroActionItem:
    # Identity
    item_id: str                        # e.g. "RETRO-2025-07-01-001"
    retro_date: date
    retro_source: RetroSource

    # What needs to happen
    description: str                    # ≥ 1 complete sentence
    done_when: str                      # verifiable completion criterion

    # Accountability
    owner: str                          # single person — email or handle
    due_date: date                      # must be ≤ next retro date of same type

    # Tracking
    status: ActionStatus = "open"
    jira_ticket: str | None = None      # required before item is assigned
    completion_evidence: str = ""       # PR, doc link, metric screenshot
    completed_date: date | None = None

    # Deferral tracking
    deferral_count: int = 0
    deferral_reason: str = ""
    new_due_date: date | None = None

    # Pattern tagging (for systemic trend detection)
    theme_tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    # e.g. ["eval-tooling", "prompt-debt", "data-access", "deployment-friction"]

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        effective_due = self.new_due_date or self.due_date
        return self.status in ("open", "in_progress") and date.today() > effective_due

    def days_open(self) -> int:
        if self.completed_date:
            return (self.completed_date - self.retro_date).days
        return (date.today() - self.retro_date).days

    def mark_done(self, evidence: str) -> None:
        if not evidence:
            raise ValueError("completion_evidence is required to close an action item")
        self.status = "done"
        self.completed_date = date.today()
        self.completion_evidence = evidence

    def defer(self, reason: str, new_due: date) -> None:
        if self.deferral_count >= 1:
            raise ValueError(
                "Maximum one deferral per action item. "
                "If work cannot proceed, cancel with a documented reason."
            )
        self.deferral_count += 1
        self.deferral_reason = reason
        self.new_due_date = new_due

    def cancel(self, reason: str) -> None:
        self.status = "cancelled"
        self.deferral_reason = reason   # reused field — cancellation reason
Opening Every Retro with Follow-Through Review

The first 10 minutes of every retrospective are non-negotiable: review the open and overdue action items from the previous retro of the same type. This is not optional and is not skipped to "save time" — it is the mechanism that makes the retro system self-reinforcing rather than self-defeating.
Follow-Through Review Script

from typing import Sequence

def open_retro_review(
    items: Sequence[RetroActionItem],
    retro_source: RetroSource,
) -> None:
    """
    Print the follow-through review summary for the opening of a retro.
    Call this before any new agenda items.
    """
    relevant = [i for i in items if i.retro_source == retro_source]

    done     = [i for i in relevant if i.status == "done"]
    overdue  = [i for i in relevant if i.is_overdue()]
    deferred = [i for i in relevant if i.status == "deferred"]
    open_ok  = [i for i in relevant if i.status in ("open", "in_progress") and not i.is_overdue()]
    cancelled = [i for i in relevant if i.status == "cancelled"]

    total = len(relevant)
    completion_rate = len(done) / total if total else 0.0

    print(f"--- {retro_source.upper()} RETRO: ACTION ITEM REVIEW ---")
    print(f"Completion rate: {completion_rate:.0%} ({len(done)}/{total})")
    print()

    if overdue:
        print(f"OVERDUE ({len(overdue)}) — discuss before new topics:")
        for item in overdue:
            print(f"  [{item.days_open()}d open] {item.description}")
            print(f"    Owner: {item.owner} | Due: {item.due_date}")
        print()

    if done:
        print(f"Done ({len(done)}):")
        for item in done:
            print(f"  ✓ {item.description}")
            print(f"    Evidence: {item.completion_evidence}")
        print()

    if deferred or cancelled:
        print(f"Deferred/Cancelled ({len(deferred) + len(cancelled)}):")
        for item in deferred + cancelled:
            print(f"  [{item.status}] {item.description} — {item.deferral_reason}")
Handling Overdue Items at the Retro Opening
Overdue items are discussed before any new topics. The discussion for each overdue item should answer three questions:
Is the item still valid? If the problem it was meant to solve no longer exists, cancel it with a note.
What is blocking completion? If there is a concrete blocker, identify it and assign an action to remove the blocker — not just to "continue working on it".

Should it be deferred or cancelled? If the team cannot commit to completing it in the next sprint, either break it into a smaller item that can be completed, or cancel it and accept the risk explicitly.

"Still working on it" is not an acceptable status. An overdue item requires a decision: scope it down, remove the blocker, or cancel it. The only purpose of leaving it open is to make the item visible — not to indefinitely carry it forward.
Action Item Health Metrics
Three metrics track the health of the action item follow-through process across retro cycles. These are reviewed at the monthly quality retro or team lead sync:
On-Time Completion Rate

def compute_completion_rate(
    items: list[RetroActionItem],
    retro_source: RetroSource | None = None,
    lookback_days: int = 90,
) -> dict:
    from datetime import timedelta
    cutoff = date.today() - timedelta(days=lookback_days)

    relevant = [
        i for i in items
        if i.retro_date >= cutoff
        and (retro_source is None or i.retro_source == retro_source)
        and i.status != "cancelled"  # cancelled items don't count against rate
    ]

    if not relevant:
        return {"rate": None, "total": 0}

    on_time = [
        i for i in relevant
        if i.status == "done"
        and i.completed_date is not None
        and i.completed_date <= (i.new_due_date or i.due_date)
    ]

    return {
        "on_time_count": len(on_time),
        "total_count": len(relevant),
        "rate": len(on_time) / len(relevant),
        "health": (
            "healthy" if len(on_time) / len(relevant) >= 0.80
            else "investigate" if len(on_time) / len(relevant) >= 0.60
            else "broken"
        ),
    }

Target: ≥ 80% on-time completion rate. If the rate falls below 60%, the retro process itself is broken — action items are too large, owners are not properly assigned, or the team does not have capacity to absorb improvement work alongside feature delivery.
Open Item Age Distribution
An action item open for more than 30 days is almost certainly not going to be completed without intervention. Track the age distribution weekly:
Deferral Rate

A deferral rate above 15% signals that action items are being created at a scope that cannot be completed in one sprint. The correction is to enforce smaller action items at creation time — not to accept chronic deferrals:
Deferral rate
Diagnosis
Corrective action
Healthy — occasional deferrals due to genuine blockers
No action needed
Action items routinely too large or capacity overcommitted
Enforce: each action item must fit in < 4 hours of effort
Retro action items are disconnected from sprint planning
Move all retro action items into the sprint backlog with proper estimation
Systemic Pattern Detection

Individual action items address specific problems. Repeat themes across multiple retros indicate systemic issues that require platform-level or process-level investment, not individual action items. Theme tags on each action item enable this analysis:

from collections import Counter

def detect_systemic_themes(
    items: list[RetroActionItem],
    min_occurrences: int = 3,
    lookback_retros: int = 6,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Find theme tags that appear 3+ times across the last N retro cycles.
    These are systemic issues requiring structural intervention.
    """
    # Get the N most recent retro dates
    retro_dates = sorted({i.retro_date for i in items}, reverse=True)
    recent_dates = set(retro_dates[:lookback_retros])

    recent_items = [i for i in items if i.retro_date in recent_dates]

    all_tags = [tag for item in recent_items for tag in item.theme_tags]
    tag_counts = Counter(all_tags)

    systemic = [
        {
            "theme": tag,
            "occurrences": count,
            "recommendation": _recommend(tag),
        }
        for tag, count in tag_counts.items()
        if count >= min_occurrences
    ]
    return sorted(systemic, key=lambda x: x["occurrences"], reverse=True)

def _recommend(theme: str) -> str:
    recommendations = {
        "eval-tooling":        "Platform investment: faster eval runner or pre-computed baselines",
        "prompt-debt":         "Schedule a prompt debt sprint; track prompt complexity metric",
        "data-access":         "Data access process review; consider self-serve eval dataset tooling",
        "deployment-friction": "CI/CD pipeline audit; target: < 30 min from merge to production",
        "unclear-criteria":    "Create a GenAI story template with mandatory eval metric field",
        "model-behavior":      "Add nightly fitness check against production traffic sample",
        "oncall-burden":       "Reduce on-call noise; audit alert thresholds; improve runbook coverage",
    }
    return recommendations.get(theme, "Escalate to architecture retro for structural review")

When a theme appears 3 or more times across the last 6 retro cycles, it is escalated to the architecture retro or cross-team forum as a platform or process investment item — no longer just an individual action item.
Integration with Sprint Planning

Retro action items that require more than 4 hours of effort must be added to the sprint backlog as first-class tickets, sized and prioritized alongside feature work. This is the mechanism that converts retro commitments into actual sprint capacity:

## Sprint Planning Integration Rule

At the start of each sprint planning session, review all open retro action items:

1. Any item due within this sprint is added to the sprint as a ticket.
   Estimate it properly (not as "a quick task").

2. Any item that will not fit in this sprint must be explicitly deferred
   (with a reason) or cancelled. "We'll get to it" is not acceptable.

3. The sprint capacity planning should reserve a slot for retro action items —
   typically 5–10% of total sprint capacity for a healthy team.
   If retro action items routinely exceed this allocation, the team is
   creating more improvement debt than it is resolving.
Summary
Retro action follow-through is a system, not a reminder. The components of a functioning system are:
Every action item has exactly one owner, a verifiable done-when criterion, a Jira ticket, and a due date before the next retro.
Every retro opens with a 10-minute follow-through review — overdue items before new topics.
Three health metrics are tracked: on-time completion rate (target ≥ 80%), open item age distribution (stale = > 30 days), and deferral rate (healthy ≤ 15%).
Theme tags enable systemic pattern detection: three occurrences of the same theme escalates to a platform or process investment.
Retro action items that require more than 4 hours are added to the sprint backlog with proper sizing — not tracked separately.

The test of a functioning retro system is simple: can the team point to a specific operational improvement — a faster eval pipeline, a fixed prompt regression, a new fitness function — that resulted directly from a retro action item from six months ago? If yes, the system is working. If not, the problem is almost always follow-through, not the quality of the retrospective discussions.
